quote: GenAI systems don't give attribution to source materials because at least as constituted now, they can't.
claim: Current generative AI architectures are structurally unable to attribute outputs to their source training materials.

quote: My guess is that none of this can easily be fixed.
claim: The copyright infringement problem in generative AI cannot be easily fixed within current architectures.

quote: Unless and until somebody can invent a new architecture that can reliably track provenance of generative text and/or generative images, infringement – often not at the request of the user — will continue.
claim: Copyright infringement in generative AI will continue until a new architecture is invented that can reliably track provenance.

quote: In all likelihood, the New York Times lawsuit is just the first of many.
claim: The NYT lawsuit against OpenAI will be followed by many similar lawsuits from other copyright holders.

quote: A good system should give the user a manifest of sources; current systems don't.
claim: Generative AI systems should provide users with a manifest of source materials used to generate outputs.

quote: Reid Southen and I continued our experiments, and wrote a long paper about what we found, and why they may pose serious problems both for users and developers that are difficult to fix.
claim: The copyright infringement problems in generative AI pose serious challenges for both users and developers that are difficult to fix.

trigger: OpenAI lobbying UK government on copyright
quote: the threat is empty: open source LLMs are already out in the wild, and a large number of people know how to build them.
claim: OpenAI's threat that enforcing copyright would kill generative AI is empty because open-source LLMs already exist and the knowledge to build them is widespread.

trigger: OpenAI lobbying UK government on copyright
quote: nobody is actually suggesting that OpenAI only use public domain works. The real suggestion, which OpenAI wants to avoid, is that they pay licensing fees for the commercial works they want to use.
claim: The copyright debate is about requiring AI companies to pay licensing fees for copyrighted training data, not about restricting them to public domain works.

trigger: OpenAI lobbying UK government on copyright
quote: It is a particular flaw of current AI — but not a logical necessity for any imaginable AI — that it cannot learn except by downloading internet-sized gobs of content
claim: Current AI's need to train on internet-scale data is a flaw of existing architectures, not a fundamental requirement for AI in general.

trigger: OpenAI lobbying UK government on copyright
quote: It's likewise a flaw that that current AI cannot learn without a tendency to inadvertently regurgitate big bits of text and images that look uncannily like their sources, but not a logical necessity.
claim: The tendency of current AI to regurgitate training data is a flaw of existing architectures, not a fundamental property of AI.

quote: the tendency towards potential infringement, in current AI looks like maybe it gets worse in bigger models.
claim: Larger generative AI models may have a greater tendency toward copyright infringement than smaller ones.

trigger: OpenAI lobbying UK government on copyright
quote: If the current batch of AI companies cannot create AI that performs reasonably well based on public domain sources and whatever they are prepared to pay to license, they should go back to the drawing board
claim: AI companies should be required to either use public domain sources or pay licensing fees, and develop architectures that do not have plagiarism problems.

quote: generative image programs are prone to exactly the same kind of regurgitation that is at issue
claim: Generative image programs exhibit the same kind of copyrighted content regurgitation as text-based LLMs.

quote: the pointer to RAG is basically a red herring. GenAI has a plagiarism problem, and it's not just a function of RAG
claim: RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) is not the primary cause of generative AI's plagiarism problem; the issue is more fundamental.

trigger: OpenAI lobbying UK government on copyright
quote: nobody will create good, fresh, new content anymore, and the internet will start eating its own tail. We will will all suffer in the end, fed a mediocre stew of regurgitate.
claim: If copyright protections are weakened for AI training, content creators will stop producing original work and the internet will degrade into recycled mediocrity.

trigger: Public Citizen petition to California AG on OpenAI nonprofit status
quote: the OpenAI drama seemed in part to be a showdown between for profit and nonprofit aspects of the organization; it is not clear that organization as whole has remained true to its nonprofit mission.
claim: OpenAI's organizational structure represents a conflict between its for-profit and nonprofit elements, and the organization may have failed to remain true to its nonprofit mission.

trigger: OpenAI reply to New York Times lawsuit
quote: 'Regurgitation' is a rare bug that we are working to drive to zero.
claim: OpenAI's claim that regurgitation is a rare bug they can drive to zero is unlikely to succeed.

trigger: OpenAI reply to New York Times lawsuit
quote: Fundamentally, virtually any guardrail has to thread a needle between the Scylla of being too restrictive and Charybdis of being too permissive. None thus far have done this effectively.
claim: AI guardrails have universally failed to balance being too restrictive versus too permissive.

trigger: OpenAI reply to New York Times lawsuit
quote: Any one example can be filtered out, but there are too many examples, and current systems are too erratic to reliably get things right.
claim: AI guardrails amount to whack-a-mole: individual examples can be filtered but the systems are too erratic to reliably prevent all violations.

trigger: OpenAI reply to New York Times lawsuit
quote: the litigation is going to be wild
claim: Litigation over AI-generated copyright infringement will be extensive.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: it is by no means 'trivial to build a classifier that detects and suppresses generated images containing copyrighted characters.'
claim: Building a classifier that reliably detects and suppresses copyrighted characters in AI-generated images is not trivial and is an unsolved problem.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: The challenge of identifying sources is not a solved problem.
claim: Identifying the copyrighted sources of AI-generated images is not a solved problem.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: It might be trivial to do it for the most popular characters, presented in the most canonical ways, but getting it right for all copyright materials is an entirely different matter.
claim: Detecting copyright infringement in AI outputs may work for the most iconic characters but fails for the long tail of copyrighted materials.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: We also speculate that any such classifier would sometimes 'false alarm' and misidentify some genuinely new materials as copyrighted.
claim: Any copyright-detection classifier for AI outputs would produce false positives, incorrectly flagging genuinely original content as copyrighted.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: almost nothing in AI is truly trivial
claim: Almost nothing in AI is truly trivial to implement reliably.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: If 2023 was the year of GenAI, 2024 is still on track to be the year of GenAI litigation.
claim: 2024 will be the year of generative AI litigation.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: still thinks that licensing is the best and only way to go
claim: Licensing copyrighted training data is the best and only viable solution to AI copyright issues.

quote: When you have terabytes of images in your training set you can generate really cool images, but that doesn't guarantee that your system will understand the relation between those images and the world.
claim: Having massive training datasets does not guarantee that generative AI systems understand the relationship between their outputs and the real world.

quote: Such errors clearly aren't disappearing any time soon.
claim: Errors of discomprehension in neural network systems will persist for a significant period.

quote: Statistical approximation through generative AI is rarely reliable.
claim: Statistical approximation through generative AI is rarely reliable.

trigger: OpenAI opening door to military applications
quote: OpenAI seems to be opening the door to military applications of their systems, and at least one well-funded startup is busy hooking up AI to military drones.
claim: Applying current AI systems to military applications like drones is dangerous because the systems lack basic comprehension of visual scenes.

quote: lawsuits against the company are likely to come fast and furious
claim: OpenAI will face a barrage of lawsuits in 2024, and defending them will consume enormous energy.

quote: battles over copyright materials may wind up cutting massively into profits
claim: Copyright battles and licensing costs will cut massively into generative AI companies' profits.

quote: OpenAI painted a false dichotomy. The choice is not between them building AI or not, it is between them building AI for free or building AI by paying for their raw materials
claim: OpenAI's claim that AI is impossible without free use of copyrighted materials is a false dichotomy; the real choice is between free and licensed use.

quote: at least so far OpenAI lacks both profits and a moat
claim: OpenAI lacks both profits and a competitive moat, as the general principles for building LLMs are well-known in the industry.

quote: Large language models such as ChatGPT may quickly become commodities; we can expect price wars, and profits may continue to be elusive, or modest at best.
claim: Large language models will become commoditized, leading to price wars and elusive profits.

quote: ChatGPT and related systems have a kind of truthiness problem; some of what they say is true, and some is not; it is very difficult for the end user to anticipate what will or not be true.
claim: LLMs like ChatGPT have a persistent truthiness problem where users cannot anticipate which outputs will be accurate.

quote: There is no immediate fix in the offing, and as someone who has studied related technologies for the last three decades, I am not certain that we will see one soon.
claim: There is no immediate fix for LLM hallucinations, and a solution may not come soon.

quote: although the underlying technology initially improved rapidly, leading to a lot of excitement, it may soon, perhaps this year or next, reach a plateau.
claim: LLM technology may reach a performance plateau in 2024 or 2025.

quote: Progress depends heavily on having vast amounts of data, and it is likely that most of the high quality data sources have already been tapped.
claim: Most high-quality training data sources for LLMs have already been tapped, limiting future progress.

quote: it is still not clear that there will be enough adoption to justify the company's stratospheric valuations
claim: It is unclear whether enterprise adoption of ChatGPT will be sufficient to justify OpenAI's stratospheric valuation.

quote: after the Fall drama some companies have lost some confidence in OpenAI
claim: The November 2023 board drama caused some enterprise customers to lose confidence in OpenAI and seek alternative vendors.

quote: OpenAI, backed by Microsoft's trillions, and now deeply tied to Microsoft, will survive, but the bloom will fade from the rose.
claim: OpenAI will survive thanks to Microsoft backing, but its perceived potential will diminish.

quote: Payouts for lawsuits and licensing will cut heavily into potential future profits.
claim: Lawsuit payouts and licensing costs will cut heavily into OpenAI's potential future profits.

quote: Valuations for OpenAI and some of its peers may drop, sharply.
claim: Valuations for OpenAI and peer generative AI companies may drop sharply.

quote: It is easier to draw a seemingly coherent image than it is to make sure that the image actually is coherent and consistent with the prompt.
claim: Generative AI image systems produce seemingly coherent images that often contain internal inconsistencies and fail to match the prompt.

quote: current systems are good at local coherence, between words, and between pixels, but not at lining up their outputs with a global comprehension of the world
claim: Current AI systems achieve local coherence between words and pixels but fail at global comprehension of the world.

quote: it is drawing from copyrighted sources and as capable as the other systems Reid Southen and I looked at in returning derivative artwork that appears to infringe without attribution
claim: Google's Bard image generation draws from copyrighted sources and produces derivative artwork that appears to infringe copyright without attribution.

quote: standing firmly by his prediction that 2024 will be the year of Generative AI litigation
claim: 2024 will be the year of generative AI litigation.

trigger: CNN report on $25M deepfake CFO scam in Hong Kong
quote: An entire cast of deepfaked people include a deep faked CFO scammed a company out $25 million.
claim: Deepfake technology is already sophisticated enough to impersonate an entire cast of people in a video conference and deceive even suspicious employees.

trigger: CNN report on $25M deepfake CFO scam in Hong Kong
quote: Now it's AI developers who are expecting to do what they do scot-free, while society picks up the tab.
claim: AI developers are creating negative externalities (like deepfake fraud) and expecting society to bear the costs, analogous to industrial pollution.

trigger: CNN report on $25M deepfake CFO scam in Hong Kong
quote: That needs to change.
claim: AI developers should be required to internalize the negative externalities their technology creates.

quote: OpenAI has misanalyzed their data in a way which underreports the potential risk.
claim: OpenAI's bioweapons risk study misapplied Bonferroni correction, inappropriately underreporting a statistically significant finding that LLMs increase accuracy for bioweapon-related tasks.

quote: if you simply collapsed all the measurements of accuracy, and did the single most obvious test here, a simple t-test, the results would (as Footnote C implies) be significant.
claim: A properly analyzed omnibus test of OpenAI's bioweapons data would show a statistically significant effect of LLM access on accuracy for bioweapon tasks.

quote: In no way whatsoever does that tell us that GPT-5 won't put someone (or someones, plural) over the threshold.
claim: Even if GPT-4 does not significantly boost bioweapons capability, future more powerful models like GPT-5 may push malicious experts over the capability threshold.

quote: If an LLM equips even one team of lunatics with the ability to build, weaponize and distribute even one pathogen as deadly as covid-19, it will be really, really big deal.
claim: The risk of LLMs enabling even one successful bioweapon attack is catastrophic enough to warrant serious concern, even if the probability is low.

quote: Another interpretation of the paper in fact is that experts were four times more likely to succeed on the pathogen formulation task with LLMs than with internet (e.g., search) alone.
claim: OpenAI's own data show experts were four times more likely to succeed on pathogen formulation with LLM access than with internet alone.

quote: the media and the public need to realize that company white papers are not peer-reviewed articles. Particularly when it comes to safety-critical questions, peer-review is essential.
claim: Company white papers on AI safety should not be treated as equivalent to peer-reviewed research; peer review is essential for safety-critical AI questions.

quote: Wholesale unintentional fabrication is not the same thing as making a flawed argument, or lying, or spinning facts for political gain
claim: LLM hallucinations are qualitatively different from human errors; LLMs fabricate wholesale while humans rarely make up outlandish false claims unintentionally.

quote: Neural networks do effectively memorize some of what they are trained on, and indeed there is a whole subfield now about the topic.
claim: Neural networks effectively memorize portions of their training data, as demonstrated by an extensive body of research on memorization and data leakage.

quote: A paper published on November 28 found that it is hard to get them to stop: 'current alignment techniques do not eliminate memorization'.
claim: Current alignment techniques do not eliminate memorization in LLMs.

quote: Given vast datasets, LLMs approximate well, but their understanding is at best superficial. That's *why* they are unreliable, and unstable, hallucinate, are constitutionally unable to fact check, etc.
claim: LLMs have at best superficial understanding despite vast training data, which is why they are unreliable, unstable, hallucinate, and cannot fact-check.

quote: anyone who fears that GPT-5 is going to convey an immense advantage in military strategy has got to be kidding.
claim: GPT-5 will not convey an immense advantage in military strategy.

quote: Special-purpose AI has been great for static, easily-simulated games with rules that have been stable for centuries, like Chess, Go, and even Diplomacy, but never been great at dynamic environments in which the environment itself is unpredictable.
claim: AI performs well in static games with stable rules but has never been great at dynamic, unpredictable real-world environments.

quote: Generative AI systems, like GPT-4, that are more general purpose, can't even handle the static situations.
claim: General-purpose generative AI systems like GPT-4 cannot even handle static game situations competently.

quote: Letting them run a war would be malpractice.
claim: Using current generative AI systems for military command decisions would be malpractice given their unreliability.

quote: he is afraid (for now) of what happens when stupid AI is over-applied
claim: The primary near-term AI risk is not from smart AI but from stupid AI being over-applied in high-stakes domains.

quote: GenAI systems do effectively store things.
claim: Generative AI systems effectively store representations from their training data, contrary to Hinton's claims.

quote: They are attracted to those effectively-stored representations, particularly (I would presume) frequent ones. They (often) don't come up with a novel independent design that would meet the requirements.
claim: Generative AI systems are attracted to frequently-seen stored representations rather than generating novel designs that match requirements.

quote: They fail to 'understand' simple concepts, such as 'design to used with only one hand.'
claim: Generative AI systems fail to understand simple functional concepts like designing a controller for one-handed use.

quote: Noncanonical situations continue to remain difficult for generative AI systems, precisely because frequent statistics are a mediocre substitute for deep understanding.
claim: Generative AI systems struggle with noncanonical situations because statistical frequency is a mediocre substitute for deep understanding.

quote: These systems don't deeply understand their inputs, contra Dr. Hinton
claim: LLMs and generative AI systems do not deeply understand their inputs.

quote: Compositionality remains a problem. So does negation.
claim: Compositionality and negation remain unsolved problems for current AI systems.

quote: Reliability remain a problem.
claim: Reliability remains an unsolved problem for current AI systems.

quote: Scale isn't everything, and hasn't and won't be enough to solve any of the problems I just listed above.
claim: Scaling alone has not and will not solve the problems of compositionality, negation, understanding, and reliability in LLMs.

quote: We really should consider trying some other approaches to AI.
claim: The AI field should explore fundamentally different approaches beyond current LLM architectures.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: The main risks here are malicious takeover of the agent and the agent making stupid mistakes.
claim: The main risks of LLM-based agents that can control user devices are malicious takeover and the agent making stupid mistakes.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: in just 14 months in the spotlight, many security issues have already come to light
claim: OpenAI has already had numerous security issues in its first 14 months, including prompt injection attacks, account takeovers, data exfiltration bugs, and Russian hacker breaches.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: Giving the keys to the castle to an LLM, at our current level of technological readiness, seems to me to be batshit insane.
claim: Giving LLM-based agents direct control over user devices is extremely reckless given the current level of AI reliability and security.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: In the very best case, LLM-based agents will change the world for the better. But I don't see that happening, certainly not this decade.
claim: LLM-based agents will not change the world for the better this decade.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: Without massive technical advances, chains of LLMs are unlikely to give us that.
claim: Chains of LLMs are unlikely to produce stable, secure AI agents without massive technical advances beyond current architectures.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: I am putting LLM-based agents distributed to millions of customers at the top of my list of most dangerous ideas of 2024.
claim: LLM-based agents distributed to millions of customers is the most dangerous AI idea of 2024.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: I am not remotely convinced that we are particularly close to AGI.
claim: We are not particularly close to achieving AGI.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: Nor am I confident that Altmann or anyone else has any idea in the near term about how to make AGI safe.
claim: No one currently knows how to make AGI safe in the near term.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: My guess is that Altman never gets the money, even in ten yearly $700B tranches.
claim: Sam Altman will not succeed in raising $7 trillion for AI infrastructure.

quote: $7 trillion worth of AI infrastructure used to full capacity would use (in precise technical terms) a metric shit ton of energy.
claim: A $7 trillion AI infrastructure buildout would consume an enormous and environmentally harmful amount of energy.

quote: So far GenAI has been mostly promise, rather then delivery. There is no argument yet that it is actually worth tearing up the globe.
claim: Generative AI has so far been mostly promise rather than delivery, and there is no argument yet that its benefits justify massive environmental costs.

quote: a colossal flop of a $7T … project could set back legitimate AI development for another decade.
claim: A failed $7 trillion AI infrastructure project could set back legitimate AI development for a decade.

quote: We should all say 'No' to reckless AI expansionism — unless and until tech leaders can show persuasively that the technology is mature enough that such expansion would demonstrably yield enormous net benefit.
claim: Society should reject massive AI infrastructure expansion until the technology is demonstrably mature enough to yield enormous net benefit.

quote: for now we should be focusing primarily on safety and innovative new approaches, rather than rushing to scale up existing and problematic architectures to unfathomable and unpredictable consequences
claim: AI investment should prioritize safety and innovative new approaches over scaling existing problematic architectures.

quote: the 'alignment problem' isn't remotely solved
claim: The AI alignment problem is far from solved.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: you don't have to be superintelligent to create serious problems
claim: AI does not need to be superintelligent to create serious problems; mediocre but widely deployed AI poses significant risks.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: I still don't think large language models have much to do with superintelligence or artificial general intelligence; I still think, with Yann LeCun, that LLMs are an 'off-ramp' on the road to AGI.
claim: LLMs are an off-ramp on the road to AGI, not a path toward superintelligence.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: I am not worried, immediately, about 'AGI risk' (the risk of superintelligent machines beyond our control), in the near term I am worried about what I will call 'MAI risk'—Mediocre AI that is unreliable (a la Bing and GPT-4) but widely deployed
claim: The near-term AI risk is not from superintelligent AGI but from Mediocre AI (MAI) that is unreliable but widely deployed with broad access.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: in many ways, the critical variable is not intelligence but power, which often caches out as access
claim: The critical variable for AI risk is not the system's intelligence but the power and access it is given to affect the real world.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: We need to stop worrying (just) about Skynet and robots taking over the world, and think a lot more about what criminals, including terrorists, might do with LLMs, and what, if anything, we might do to stop them.
claim: AI risk discourse should focus more on criminal and terrorist misuse of LLMs and less exclusively on superintelligence scenarios.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: AI may well directly or indirectly accelerate many of these risks (e.g., misinformation, future pandemics, rogue nuclear weapon launches, misuse of CRISPR, etc).
claim: AI may directly or indirectly accelerate multiple existential risks including misinformation, pandemics, nuclear weapons misuse, and CRISPR misuse.

trigger: Sam Altman X post defending $7T ask
quote: How do we know that $7 trillion invested into LLMs and their infrastructure would not simply exacerbate those costs, grinding down content creators, women, and the environment, undermining democracy, destroying jobs, etc?
claim: A $7 trillion investment into LLM infrastructure could exacerbate harms to content creators, the environment, democracy, and employment rather than securing humanity's future.

trigger: Sam Altman X post defending $7T ask
quote: The risk of premature commitment looms large.
claim: There is a significant risk of premature commitment to scaling current AI technology before it is ready.

quote: In some ways we have made progress, but in the ones I keep harping on—factuality and compositionality—we have not.
claim: Despite overall progress in AI image generation, factuality and compositionality have not meaningfully improved from DALL-E 2 through DALL-E 3 and Gemini Ultra.

quote: the problem lies not in Dall-E's drawing ability, but in its ability to cope with noncanonical prompts.
claim: AI image generation failures stem from poor language understanding rather than limitations in drawing capability.

quote: the problem was with its language understanding, rather than with illustration per se
claim: The core limitation of AI image generators is language understanding, not illustration ability.

quote: Generative AI systems have always tried to use statistics as a proxy for deeper understanding, and it's never entirely worked.
claim: Generative AI systems use statistics as a proxy for deeper understanding, and this approach has never entirely worked.

quote: statistically improbable requests like horses riding astronauts have always been challenging for generative image systems
claim: Statistically improbable prompts remain challenging for generative image systems because they rely on frequency rather than understanding.

quote: statistical frequency (most guitarists play righthanded) won out over linguistic understanding
claim: Generative AI systems default to statistically frequent outputs over linguistically specified ones, showing reliance on training data frequency over language understanding.

quote: Yet again, the statistics outweigh understanding. The foundation remains shaky.
claim: Foundation models remain unreliable because they rely on statistical patterns rather than genuine understanding.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: the truth is it doesn't work that well yet
claim: Programming in natural language via LLMs does not work well yet despite the appeal of the concept.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: If the system really is being 'programmed in English' it is not always doing what is being asked to do
claim: LLMs programmed via English-language system prompts frequently fail to follow their own instructions.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: writing your program in English 'prompts' is fast upfront, but downstream may lead to serious challenges in debugging
claim: Programming via English prompts may be fast initially but creates serious downstream debugging challenges.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: We have apparently just entered a new and different and frightening era, in which debugging involves not only fixing our own mistakes but coming to terms with machines that may or may not uphold their end of the bargain.
claim: The era of LLM-assisted programming introduces a new debugging challenge where machines may not reliably execute instructions, unlike deterministic programming.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: there is an argument that the quality of code has declined, in the era in which large language models have been used as copilots
claim: Code quality may have declined since LLMs began being used as coding copilots.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: The reason that software engineering is done in formal languages is not because formal languages make it harder to specify what you want than English. Obviously. It's because formal languages make it much, much easier to specify what you want.
claim: Formal programming languages exist because they make it much easier to specify intent precisely than natural language, not because they are harder to use.

quote: If other judges rule the same way, and hold companies responsible for the nonsense their chatbots say, one of the biggest alleged use cases for large language models could dry up, fast.
claim: If courts hold companies liable for chatbot confabulations, the customer service use case for LLMs could collapse

quote: an Air Canada chatbot confabulated ("hallucinated") an answer – telling a customer that bereavement trips would be refunded
claim: LLM-powered chatbots confabulate answers in customer service contexts, making up policies that do not exist

quote: We are about to have a whole generation of children educated by fake videos that are completely plausible to naive audiences yet biologically (or physically or culturally, etc) incorrect.
claim: AI-generated video will produce a generation of children exposed to plausible but biologically, physically, or culturally incorrect educational content

quote: In short order, with Sora or some opensource counterparts that will emerge, scammers will start to make photorealistic fake videos simply to sell ads.
claim: Scammers will soon use Sora-like tools to create photorealistic fake videos for ad revenue

quote: A major educational resource will likely be spoiled practically overnight.
claim: YouTube as an educational resource will be degraded by AI-generated fake videos

quote: Probably the biggest single problem with large language models is that they make things up
claim: The biggest single problem with LLMs is confabulation

quote: Turns out Sora makes stuff up, too
claim: Sora exhibits the same confabulation problem as LLMs, but in the visual domain

quote: The error is not an error of insufficient computational power
claim: Sora's errors in rendering chess boards and similar artifacts are not due to insufficient compute

quote: Nowadays, we have "multimodal" systems, trained on both text and images, and the aforementioned "hallucinations" … and other errors persist and even crop up in new ways.
claim: Adding multimodal grounding (images + text) has not solved LLM confabulation problems

quote: Sora is failing to apprehend a cultural regularity of the world, despite ample evidence
claim: Sora fails to learn cultural regularities like the rules of chess despite ample training data

quote: The system is trying to approximate the world, but it just isn't very good at that job. It uses arrangements of pixels to predict other arrangements of pixels, but it does not try to build an internal model of physics, and it does not try to build an internal model of cultural artifacts, either.
claim: Sora approximates pixel arrangements rather than building internal models of physics or cultural artifacts

quote: In most or all of the errors we have seen thus far, virtually every bit of the images are locally correct; if you just looked at a tiny patch of a given flawed video in isolation, for a single moment, you wouldn't notice anything wrong. What's missing is any global comprehension by the system.
claim: Sora's outputs are locally correct at the pixel level but lack global comprehension of scenes

quote: From AGI perspective, however, this is an immense warning sign. The factuality problems that have haunted LLMs are present even in a new architecture trained on ungodly amounts of data.
claim: Sora's factuality problems despite massive data are a warning sign that new architectures and more data do not solve confabulation for AGI

quote: In my view, these claims — about AGI and world models — are hyperbolic, and unlikely to be true.
claim: Claims that Sora represents progress toward AGI or true world models are hyperbolic and unlikely to be true

quote: OpenAI wants us to believe that this is a "path towards building general purpose simulations of the physical world". As it turns out, that's either hype or confused
claim: OpenAI's claim that Sora is a path to general-purpose physical world simulation is hype or confused

quote: children may be born with object permanence, and certainly have some control it by the age of 4 or 5 months; Sora is never really getting it, even with mountains and mountains of data.
claim: Sora fails at basic object permanence despite massive training data, something human infants achieve by 4-5 months

quote: I predict that many will be hard to remedy. Why? Because the glitches don't stem from the data, they stem from a flaw in how the system reconstructs reality.
claim: Sora's physics glitches will be hard to fix because they stem from fundamental flaws in how the system reconstructs reality, not from data insufficiency

quote: these glitches are in some ways akin to LLM "hallucinations", artifacts from (roughly speaking) decompression from lossy compression. They don't derive from the world.
claim: Sora's physics glitches are analogous to LLM hallucinations -- artifacts of lossy compression rather than reflections of training data

quote: More data won't solve that problem. And like other generative AI systems, there is no way to encode (and guarantee) constraints like "be truthful" or "obey the laws of physics" or "don't just invent (or eliminate) objects".
claim: More data will not solve Sora's physics problems, and there is no way to guarantee constraints like truthfulness or physics compliance in generative AI

quote: Indeed the real lesson here is that Generative AI remains a recalcitrant beast, no matter how much data you throw at it.
claim: Generative AI remains fundamentally unreliable regardless of data scale

quote: Sora is not a solution to AI's longstanding woes with space, time, and causality.
claim: Sora does not solve AI's longstanding problems with space, time, and causality

quote: If a system can't at all handle with the permanence of objects, I am not sure we should even call it a world model at all.
claim: A system that cannot handle object permanence should not be called a world model

quote: Sora is fantastic, but it is akin to morphing and splicing, rather than a path to the physical reasoning we would need for AGI. It is a model of how images change over time, not a model of what entities do in the world.
claim: Sora models how images change over time, not how entities behave in the world, making it irrelevant to AGI

quote: As a solution to artificial general intelligence, though, I see it as a distraction.
claim: Sora is a distraction from, not a path toward, AGI

quote: The need for altogether different technologies that are less opaque, more interpretable, more maintanable, and more debuggable — and hence more tractable—remains paramount.
claim: AI needs fundamentally different technologies that are less opaque, more interpretable, more maintainable, and more debuggable than current LLMs

quote: Generative AI is a kind of alchemy
claim: Generative AI development is a kind of alchemy -- tinkering without deep understanding

quote: these systems have never been been stable. Nobody has ever been able to engineer safety guarantees around then.
claim: Generative AI systems have never been stable and nobody has been able to engineer safety guarantees around them

quote: The AI we have got now isn't really up to the job. Getting the delicate balance here right is way outside current AI's wheelhouse.
claim: Current AI is not capable of balancing diversity representation with historical accuracy in image generation

quote: What one wants is a system that can distinguish past from future, acknowledging history while also playing a role in moving towards a more positive future. And one wants the system to have a nuanced understanding of what is even been asked. (That's the clearly lacking now).
claim: Current AI systems lack the nuanced understanding needed to distinguish historical from contemporary contexts in queries

quote: Some things have scaled exponentially, and some haven't. The capacity to endow machines with the commonsense understanding of history, culture, and human values that would be required to make sensible guardrails has not.
claim: The ability to give AI commonsense understanding of history, culture, and human values has not scaled with compute and data

quote: Maybe, and I am just spitballing, LLMs aren't quite the magic panacea that the industry lead us all to believe?
claim: LLMs are not the transformative panacea the industry has claimed

quote: Sora's can't reliably handle basic physics, ChatGPT had an unexplained meltdown, Gemini can't even remember who was on Apollo 11, there are no formal guarantees that any of this will ever work
claim: There are no formal guarantees that generative AI systems will ever work reliably

quote: The road from research idea to reliable production remains steep. RAG may reduce hallucinations somewhat, but it certainly won't come close to eliminating them.
claim: RAG may reduce LLM hallucinations somewhat but will not come close to eliminating them

quote: To make RAG work reliably would be to solve AGI.
claim: Making RAG work reliably for all queries is as hard as solving AGI

quote: To reliably address all queries, and not just some obvious ones like what was the recent Super Bowl score, would require solutions all the things the field has been struggling to do for 75 years without success.
claim: Reliably answering all queries via RAG would require solving problems AI has failed to solve for 75 years

quote: RAG would need to take natural language and visual input and deconstruct it a logical, computer interpretable form that you can check against databases, and reason over the results. That's really hard.
claim: For RAG to work reliably it would need to decompose natural language into logical forms and reason over them, which is extremely difficult

quote: Imagining perfectly reliable, hallucination-free chatbots by this time next year is bonkers.
claim: Perfectly reliable, hallucination-free chatbots will not exist by early 2025

quote: I do think AI will eventually work reliably. Some great-grandchild of RAG, perhaps extended, revised, and elaborated, might even help — if people seriously embrace neurosymbolic AI in coming years.
claim: AI will eventually work reliably, likely requiring neurosymbolic approaches rather than current LLM/RAG methods

quote: Making it work is very likely going to require a whole new technology, one that is unlikely to arrive until the tech industry gets over its infatuation with LLMs and quick fixes.
claim: Reliable AI will require a fundamentally new technology that will not arrive until the industry moves past its LLM fixation

quote: Waiting on RAG to fix what ails LLM is just waiting on another miracle.
claim: RAG is not going to fix the fundamental problems with LLMs

quote: sound advice for the particular circumstance (sternotomy) has been combined with generic advice (about ergonomics) that would sensible in other circumstances but deeply problematic here
claim: GenAI search engines produce dangerous medical errors by combining generically correct advice in contextually inappropriate ways (generative pastiche)

quote: New results from Subbarao Kambhapati cast doubts on the robustness of chain of thought prompting
claim: Chain-of-thought prompting is not robust, based on Kambhapati's research

quote: OpenAI as a whole no longer works according to the mission that was originally set out.
claim: OpenAI no longer operates according to its original nonprofit mission

quote: Sora has not learned physics, and is not a reliable guide to how the world works. It is at best a semi-reliable guide to how the world looks.
claim: Sora has not learned physics; it is at best a semi-reliable guide to how the world looks, not how it works

quote: These two—how the world works, and how the world looks—are fundamentally different.
claim: Modeling how the world looks (pixel prediction) is fundamentally different from modeling how the world works (physics)

quote: Sora absolutely fails at object permanence 101. People, animals, limbs, etc routinely wink in and out of existence willy-nilly, as a function of predictions about pixels, rather than as the computations of the states of physical entities.
claim: Sora fails at basic object permanence because it predicts pixels rather than computing states of physical entities

quote: It is precisely because Sora is not a physics engine that a large fraction of Sora's videos are inconsistent with physics or biology, in very basic ways.
claim: A large fraction of Sora's videos are inconsistent with basic physics or biology because Sora is not a physics engine

quote: Whenever there is a conflict between physics and image sequence prediction, image sequence prediction wins. The laws of physics are about objects, not pixels; Sora knows only about pixels and image spaces, not objects.
claim: When physics conflicts with image prediction in Sora, image prediction always wins because Sora operates on pixels, not objects

quote: humanity has now taken a back seat to money; their choices now seem very directly anchored in a need to generate a massive return, in direct contradiction to their promises to California, to Musk, and to the world.
claim: OpenAI now prioritizes profit over its original humanitarian mission, contradicting its promises

trigger: Hofmann et al. covert racism paper
quote: LLMs are, as I have been trying to tell you, too stupid to understand concepts like people and race; their fealty to superficial statistics drives this horrific stereotyping
claim: LLMs lack genuine understanding of concepts like people and race; their reliance on superficial statistics drives covert racist stereotyping

trigger: Hofmann et al. covert racism paper
quote: scale actually makes the covert racism worse
claim: Scaling up LLMs makes covert racism worse, not better

trigger: Hofmann et al. covert racism paper
quote: I honestly don't see an easy fix — simply adding human feedback made things worse, as the authors showed. And Google's debacle shows that guardrails are never easy.
claim: There is no easy fix for covert racism in LLMs; RLHF makes it worse and guardrails are unreliable

trigger: Hofmann et al. covert racism paper
quote: the LLM companies should recall their systems until they can find an adequate solution
claim: LLM companies should recall their systems until covert racism is adequately addressed

trigger: Hofmann et al. covert racism paper
quote: I would encourage Congress, the EEOC, HUD, the FTC, and others to investigate, and demand user logs and interaction data from the major LLM manufacturers.
claim: US government agencies should investigate LLM covert racism and demand user interaction data from LLM manufacturers

quote: it shows decisively that OpenAI knew for years that they had no intention of being as open as their name or public statements suggested.
claim: OpenAI knew for years it had no intention of being as open as its name and public statements suggested

quote: the apparent copyright infringements that Reid Southen and I discussed in early January continue apace two months later
claim: OpenAI's apparent copyright infringement in image generation continues unaddressed

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: Scaling has undeniably helped, but not solved any of the problems I pointed to above.
claim: Scaling has helped AI performance but has not solved problems of comprehension, factuality, stability, or reasoning

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: We are still a long way from machines that can genuinely understand human language
claim: Current AI systems are still far from genuinely understanding human language

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: we nowhere near the ordinary day-to-day intelligence of Rosey the Robot, a science-fiction housekeeper that could not only interpret a wide variety of human requests but safely act on them in real time.
claim: Current AI is nowhere near the general-purpose domestic robot intelligence depicted in science fiction

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: In time we will see that deep learning was only a tiny part of what we need to build if we're ever going to get trustworthy AI
claim: Deep learning will turn out to be only a small part of what is needed for trustworthy AI

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: Deep learning, which is fundamentally a technique for recognizing patterns, is at its best when all we need are rough-ready results, where stakes are low and perfect results optional
claim: Deep learning is best suited for low-stakes tasks where approximate results are acceptable

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: Current deep-learning systems frequently succumb to stupid errors.
claim: Current deep learning systems frequently make basic errors

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: scaling laws aren't universal laws like gravity but rather mere observations that might not hold forever, much like Moore's law
claim: AI scaling laws are empirical observations that may not hold indefinitely, analogous to Moore's law

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: I still think we need a paradigm shift. And it is increasingly looking like LLMs on their own are not the answer to AGI
claim: LLMs alone are not the answer to AGI; a paradigm shift is needed

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: the measures that have scaled have not captured what we desperately need to improve: genuine comprehension
claim: The metrics that have improved with scaling do not capture genuine comprehension

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: Toxic language can easily be elicited by jailbreaks, but less so in ordinary use; misinformation is still common, even without jailbreaks
claim: LLM toxicity has improved in normal use but remains elicitable via jailbreaks; misinformation remains common even without jailbreaks

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: people like Bill Gates, Demis Hassabis, Yann LeCun, and Sam Altman have all recognized that a plateau might be coming
claim: Even prominent AI leaders (Gates, Hassabis, LeCun, Altman) have recognized that AI scaling may be hitting a plateau

quote: why $7T, if the ROI isn't yet clear?
claim: The ROI on generative AI investment is not yet clear despite massive spending

trigger: Retracted rat paper with AI-generated images
quote: GenAI is messing with science. And it may get a lot worse.
claim: Generative AI is degrading scientific integrity and the problem may worsen significantly

trigger: Retracted rat paper with AI-generated images
quote: the long term consequences on science may be severe
claim: The long-term consequences of LLM use in scientific papers may be severe

trigger: Retracted rat paper with AI-generated images
quote: GenAI-created garbage could quickly overwhelm their reviewing processes, leading to a precipitous drop in reputation. Everyone in science loses.
claim: GenAI-generated garbage submissions could overwhelm journal review processes, damaging scientific publishing reputation

trigger: Mira Murati WSJ interview on Sora training data
quote: Aside from all that, Murati claimed she had no idea what specific data Sora was trained on; given her role as CTO that seems all but inconceivable.
claim: OpenAI CTO Mira Murati's claim of not knowing what data Sora was trained on is not credible given her role

trigger: Mira Murati WSJ interview on Sora training data
quote: Half truths about openness, about what data they are training on, about finances, about their views on regulation, about when if ever they would pause, and more, essentially every topic that matters, from the day they were launched all the way to the present. A lack of full candor seems to run straight through the corporate DNA.
claim: A pattern of half-truths and lack of candor on openness, data, finances, and regulation runs through OpenAI's corporate DNA

trigger: Discovery of ChatGPT remnant phrases in scientific papers
quote: every article with ChatGPT remnants should be considered suspect and perhaps retracted, because hallucinations may have filtered in, and both authors and reviewers) were asleep at the switch.
claim: Every scientific article containing ChatGPT remnant phrases should be considered suspect and possibly retracted because hallucinations may have infiltrated the content

trigger: Discovery of ChatGPT remnant phrases in scientific papers
quote: Reviewers are typically unpaid academics who are already stretched to their limits; tripling their workload would not be feasible. And GenAI might do a lot worse than merely tripling workloads; the total number of articles may radically spike, many of them dubious and a waste of reviewers' time.
claim: GenAI will cause a radical spike in dubious scientific paper submissions that overwhelms the already-strained peer review system

trigger: Discovery of ChatGPT remnant phrases in scientific papers
quote: If science journals and science itself are overrun by LLM-generated garbage, as now seems imminent, my agnostic "I can't quite tell yet if LLMs will ultimately be of net benefit to society" is going to switch to "shut it down if they can't fix this problem."
claim: Science journals being overrun by LLM-generated garbage is imminent and may tip the cost-benefit analysis of LLMs to net negative for society

trigger: Discovery of ChatGPT remnant phrases in scientific papers
quote: Literally the only other solution I can envision is to force LLM manufacturers to pay a significant tax to subsidize the journal review system that they are about to destroy.
claim: LLM manufacturers should be taxed to subsidize the journal review system that their products are degrading

quote: We might be reaching a plateau in terms of sheer capability. Nobody has been able to beat it decisively.
claim: AI capabilities may be reaching a plateau at the GPT-4 level, with no competitor decisively surpassing it after one year.

quote: Places like Google and Anthropic have put in a lot of money trying. None succeeded; instead there tentatively seems to be convergence at GPT-4 levels.
claim: Google and Anthropic have spent heavily but converged at GPT-4-level performance rather than surpassing it.

quote: putting LLMs into production is hard; most work to date has been preliminary. Companies are starting to temper their expectations. Many initial expectations were unrealistic.
claim: Most enterprise LLM deployments have been preliminary rather than production-level, and initial corporate expectations for LLMs were unrealistic.

quote: Bad actors, who may have lower standards for reliability, appear to be using them for cybercrime and disinformation.
claim: Bad actors are using LLMs for cybercrime and disinformation because they have lower reliability standards than legitimate users.

quote: Some core problems have lingered, particularly hallucinations, instability and occasional nonsensical errors.
claim: Core LLM problems including hallucinations, instability, and nonsensical errors remain unsolved after one year of GPT-4.

quote: OpenAI was here first, but there's not a huge moat; many other players appear to be catching up. We can expect price wars.
claim: OpenAI has no significant competitive moat in LLMs, and price wars among LLM providers are expected.

quote: Profitability remains in question. Big models cost big $, and getting standout results might cost huge $; licensing and legal costs may be substantial.
claim: The profitability of large language models remains questionable due to high training, operating, licensing, and legal costs.

trigger: OpenAI VP Anna Makanju remarks at Columbia
quote: the inherent unreliability in these systems is deeply worrisome
claim: The inherent unreliability of LLM-based AI systems is a serious concern that undermines their positive applications.

quote: If lots of code gets written, fast, but that code is riddled with security problems, the net advantage on the positive side of the ledger may be less than anticipated.
claim: AI-generated code may produce a net negative due to security vulnerabilities, offsetting productivity gains.

quote: I am increasingly worried that the big tech companies are—in conjunction with a Congress that has not yet passed any substantive AI legislation—successfully architecting a grossly asymmetric world, in which the profits from the positive side flow to them, and the costs of the negative side are borne by everyone else.
claim: Big tech companies are structuring the AI ecosystem so that they capture the profits while society bears the costs of AI harms.

quote: If the 2024 election flips based on GenAI-created disinformation, there will be a huge cost to society, but it is unlikely that the GenAI companies will be held responsible in any serious way.
claim: If GenAI-created disinformation influences the 2024 election, AI companies are unlikely to be held legally responsible.
